And they were much more strategic in how they looked at everything. So I try to be very strategic. I question the business model. I look at the front end, the back end. I look at where there's gaps. When you look at the three-way to grow business model, most people, and I suspect you're much different because, and I'm not flattering them, but because you're exposure, but most people spend all their time, money, effort bringing in buyers and do nothing with it. When it's so much easier to get the average sale up ethically, get the buyers buying far more often when you have nothing else to sell, there are a myriad of very ethical ways to continuously and perpetually monetize it and turn one or two or five times sales in it. I look at, I mean, I've done hundreds of millions of dollars worth of reactivation. And I do a lot of stuff like that. We have, and I apologize because I got so many sort of template things. I don't always remember them, but we do things that are based on the highest and best use theory. In other words, same effort, many times more results, same opportunity, many times more results now and in the future. And we basically, there's like nine leverage points we work on. One is certainly marketing, one is strategy, one is ideology, one is distribution, one is capital, you know, your financial capital, your human capital, your intellectual capital. I am probably best known and I just came from doing a very intensive program for doing all kinds of strategic alliances, joint ventures, partnering, endorsement deals, because like we were teaching people how to become deal makers and marketing consultants and they wanted to add to run like that. Well, I ran those earlier in my life, but I said, why do you do that? If you can find somebody who's already got access to the market and can call, write or take you there and you got the best, the goodwill. And so it's like, I try to shorten the timeline and eliminate the wastage, but we question, we have about 150 different areas we look at, but three way to grow a business model, a concept called the power parthenon, which is basing your business on multiple sources. I have a very powerful strategy that I evolve very frankly after I started working with Phillips Publishing. I learned it from them and evolved it. It's called the strategy of preeminence and it's a way of distinguishing yourself, your business, your product and an unimpeachably higher level than being seen as the only trusted advisor. And it's got a lot of elements to it. One of them is you have a moral obligation not to let somebody not buy from you if they're going to be better off, not to let them buy less, less quantity, quality, combination, frequency. And it's a very liberating concept that we use as a fundamental, like a string that goes between everything. It's the culture, the philosophy, all the marketing we do for people. I mean, I don't know. It's like, I had so many. So let's, so let's. You can bring me more granular if you like. So let's just jump to a case history. Let's just jump to a story. The biggest, the best example. The dumbest one you've ever mumbled into. Sure. I'll give you a couple of quick ones. I'll give you three because you asked me something before. I've got a great story about Gary Halbert and Chase Revelle, which is. Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. I have a good Chase story. Well, I got one about the two of them, which is pretty interesting. Oh, yeah, we got to have that. So, so. I heard that. Well, I, you know, I got a couple of claims to FAMES and they're all three very cool stories, but they talk about marketing strategy, business model, repurposing. And I think I can do them all quickly. The first one was we did this product called Ice Yacht. It's my supposedly claimed to fame and the company had no money and they were selling a product for $3 and nobody ever looked at the lifetime value. And we realized the lifetime value was a mail order product way, way back. I am older. I'm 60. It was 45 cents for the product in the mail bulk shipped. That's how cheap it was. And it was a $3 glob of gelatinous goop that was used to treat arthritis. Nobody had looked at the lifetime value. When we did, we found that about 80% of the buyers bought every month. Literally forever or until somebody came up with a cure for arthritis, precise, or so they died. And with that piece of, the guys still didn't have any money, but we were able to go to all over the place to radio stations, television stations, magazines, and get a thousand of them to run our ads on a pure performance because we let them keep 100% of the money. We could have let them keep three times that and paid them on it because we got our money back in the court before the first month was over. 80% reordered and about 30% upgraded to one of 40 other products. And then we tried a lot of things, but we were able to use that concept and we did some really interesting strategy. We gave 100% to the media, but again, we were making, every time we sold a $3 jar and lost 45 cents, we were making $28 a year for life.
